---
title: "Day 3 — Exercise: Parsing a region string with `Result`"
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    code-copy: true
    code-overflow: wrap
    highlight-style: github
---

## Goal

Write `parse_region(s: &str) -> Result<(String, u64, u64), ParseRegionError>`: parse a region string like `"chr1:1000-2000"` into a chromosome name and a half-open coordinate pair. Define a custom error enum `ParseRegionError` so each failure mode is its own value, not a string message.

This is your first encounter with **fallible parsing as a value**: `Result` represents "this might fail, here is how" without throwing exceptions and without dropping you out of the function.

## Background

Region strings of the form `chrom:start-end` appear everywhere — `samtools view`, `bcftools view`, `tabix`, IGV. Five things can go wrong:

1. No colon separating the chromosome from the coordinates.
2. No dash separating start from end.
3. The start is not a valid number.
4. The end is not a valid number.
5. The end is less than the start.

Each failure deserves its own variant in a [`ParseRegionError`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/book/ch06-01-defining-an-enum.html) enum, so the caller can decide what to do.

## The exercise

```bash
cd day3/ex-parse-region
```

Define `ParseRegionError` and `parse_region`. Make all eight tests pass.

## How to run

```bash
cargo test
cargo run -- chr1:1000-2000    # OK: chr1 from 1000 to 2000 (length 1000)
cargo run -- bogus             # error: MissingColon
cargo run -- chr1:abc-2000     # error: InvalidStart
```

## Hints

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" title="Hint 1 — defining the error enum"}
A C-style [`enum`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/book/ch06-01-defining-an-enum.html) listing all five failure modes. Derive `Debug`, `Clone`, `PartialEq`:

```rust
#[derive(Debug, Clone, PartialEq, Eq)]
enum ParseRegionError {
    MissingColon,
    MissingDash,
    InvalidStart,
    InvalidEnd,
    EndBeforeStart,
}
```
:::

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" title="Hint 2 — splitting the string"}
[`str::split_once`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/std/primitive.str.html#method.split_once) returns an [`Option<(&str, &str)>`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/std/option/enum.Option.html) — `Some` if the delimiter was found, `None` otherwise. Combine with [`Option::ok_or`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/std/option/enum.Option.html#method.ok_or) to convert "no delimiter" into a `Result` failure, then propagate with the [`?` operator](https://doc.rust-lang.org/book/ch09-02-recoverable-errors-with-result.html#a-shortcut-for-propagating-errors-the--operator):

```rust
let (chrom, rest) = s.split_once(':').ok_or(ParseRegionError::MissingColon)?;
let (start_s, end_s) = rest.split_once('-').ok_or(ParseRegionError::MissingDash)?;
```

`?` is a shortcut for "if it is `Err`, return it from the enclosing function; otherwise unwrap to the inner value and continue".
:::

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" title="Hint 3 — parsing the numbers"}
[`str::parse`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/std/primitive.str.html#method.parse) returns a `Result` whose error type is decided by the destination type. Convert that error to our error enum with [`Result::map_err`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/std/result/enum.Result.html#method.map_err), then `?` it:

```rust
let start: u64 = start_s.parse().map_err(|_| ParseRegionError::InvalidStart)?;
let end:   u64 = end_s  .parse().map_err(|_| ParseRegionError::InvalidEnd)?;
```

The `|_|` is a [closure](https://doc.rust-lang.org/book/ch13-01-closures.html) that ignores the inner parse error — we only care that parsing failed, not why.
:::

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" title="Hint 4 — the final check"}
Once `start` and `end` are real numbers, verify the order. Return `Err` explicitly here (no `?`):

```rust
if end < start {
    return Err(ParseRegionError::EndBeforeStart);
}
Ok((chrom.to_string(), start, end))
```

[`Ok`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/std/result/enum.Result.html#variant.Ok) wraps the success value. Note `chrom.to_string()` — `chrom` is a `&str` borrowing from the input, but the returned `String` needs to be owned so it can outlive the parse.
:::

## Solution

::: {.callout-note collapse="true" title="Solution"}
```rust
#[derive(Debug, Clone, PartialEq, Eq)]
enum ParseRegionError {
    MissingColon,
    MissingDash,
    InvalidStart,
    InvalidEnd,
    EndBeforeStart,
}

fn parse_region(s: &str) -> Result<(String, u64, u64), ParseRegionError> {
    let (chrom, rest) = s.split_once(':').ok_or(ParseRegionError::MissingColon)?;
    let (start_s, end_s) = rest.split_once('-').ok_or(ParseRegionError::MissingDash)?;
    let start: u64 = start_s.parse().map_err(|_| ParseRegionError::InvalidStart)?;
    let end:   u64 = end_s  .parse().map_err(|_| ParseRegionError::InvalidEnd)?;
    if end < start {
        return Err(ParseRegionError::EndBeforeStart);
    }
    Ok((chrom.to_string(), start, end))
}
```

Full reference in `day3/ex-parse-region-solution/src/main.rs`.
:::

## Where this leads

- **Day 4**: parse a whole BED file line-by-line using this pattern. Errors will accumulate in a `Vec<ParseRegionError>` or short-circuit on the first one — *you* will decide, because `Result` makes it explicit.
- Many real Rust libraries use `thiserror` to derive `std::error::Error` and `Display` impls for custom error enums like this one. We will mention it on day 4 but not require it.
